publisher.publisher-name: Association for Research in Vision and Ophthalmology

title-group.article-title: Prognostic Ability of Automated RPE Sinuosity for Late AMD Supersedes Qualitative Outer Retinal Band Assessment
title-group.alt-title: Outer Retinal Bands for AMD Prognostication

contrib-group.aff: 1School of Optometry and Vision Science, New South Wales, Australia
2Centre for Eye Health, University of New South Wales, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia
3Vision Eye Institute, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia
4The University of Sydney, Faculty of Medicine and Health, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence: Lisa Nivison-Smith, School of Optometry and Vision Science, University of New South Wales Australia, Sydney, New South Wales 2052, Australia; l.nivison-smith@unsw.edu.au.

license.license-p: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License.

sec.title: Purpose
sec.p: Outer retinal band integrity strongly predicts late age-related macular degeneration (AMD), however, it is often assessed subjectively and vulnerable to intergrader variability. This study quantifies retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) sinuosity from automated segmentations of a commercially available optical coherence tomography (OCT) and its ability to predict late AMD.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Consecutive patients with intermediate AMD who progressed to late disease (n = 69) or remained stable (n = 198) were recruited. Outer retinal band integrity was measured quantitatively using RPE sinuosity, calculated as the ratio of RPE:RPE-fit line length across macular B-scans. RPE sinuosity was measured in the foveal B-scan alongside the mean, maximum, and median values across the five central B-scans. The RPE and ellipsoid zone (EZ) continuity was graded qualitatively, as well as other AMD biomarkers currently used for prognostication. Adjusted odds ratios for progression were calculated for each variable.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Mean follow-up time was 3.15 years (1.93 years). Pigmentary abnormalities (P = 0.001), subretinal drusenoid deposits (SDDs; P > 0.0001), drusen volume (P = 0.01), and late AMD in the fellow eye (P = 0.001) were present in a higher proportion of progressor than stable eyes at baseline. Median RPE sinuosity was the strongest predictor of progression within 5 years (2.52 [1.14–5.55]) and across the study period (2.35 [1.25–4.43]). No qualitative measures could predict late AMD within any time frame.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: RPE sinuosity outperformed qualitative outer retinal band integrity assessments for late AMD prediction and is a reliable, repeatable evaluation method. Verifying these results using different OCT devices in a prospective cohort is needed to determine whether the feature can enhance late AMD risk models and improve clinical management.

body.p: Recent work shows that integrity of the outer retinal bands, including the external limiting membrane, ellipsoid zone (EZ), interdigitation zone, and retinal pigment epithelium (RPE), on OCT, are strong predictors of late age-related macular degeneration (AMD) development, and outperform other established predictors such as drusen volume and intraretinal hyper-reflective foci.1,2 However, there have been mixed reports about the reliability of these assessments which limits their prognostic value. Outer retinal band integrity is typically assessed by subjectively classifying them as “complete” or “disrupted” from the foveal B-scan on optical coherence tomography (OCT).2–4 Although good reliability has been reported,5,6 others have found it to be suboptimal7,8 and affected by the experience of raters.9 Indeed, some have reported the κ value of these gradings to be less than 0.6,7,10 which is considered inadequate for clinical purposes.11

An automatic measure of outer retinal integrity that is free of subjective bias could improve the reliability of assessments and implementation in community eyecare settings, particularly when expert review is unavailable. Existing quantitative assessment methods segment the EZ area from en face images of OCT volumes12 and calculate the reflectivity of outer retinal bands against a reference feature (e.g., the external limiting membrane and vitreous). Whereas there is less reliance on subjective grading, these measures are still vulnerable to human error and variations as segmentation, and sampling of reference areas are performed manually.13,14 Artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms have also been explored to calculate outer retinal band thickness and reflectivity,13,15 however, they could be difficult to implement in clinical practice as necessary technological infrastructure may not be readily available. The labor-intensive process of curating labeled data sets to train AI models for retinal biomarker identification is also a significant barrier to implementation.16,17

Previous work by our group has shown that outer retinal band curvature can be used to quantify outer retinal band integrity for AMD screening18 and prognostication.19 Specifically, RPE curvature was defined as the ratio of the RPE band length to Bruch's membrane length to calculate the sinuosity, which measures tortuosity. We found that baseline RPE sinuosity exceeding 3% was a stronger predictor for late AMD development within 5 years compared with established AMD biomarkers – drusen volume, pigmentary abnormalities, and subretinal drusenoid deposits (SDDs).19 Although these results are promising, manual segmentation was still required as proof-of-concept to ensure that increased sinuosity is genuinely associated with greater outer retinal pathology in AMD, and to evaluate its potential as a summative biomarker for screening and prognosis. Verifying the proposed biomarker using easily accessible, automated segmentations is therefore needed to determine its translatability for improving clinical assessments and potentially, AI decision support tools. Its application to a larger, high-risk cohort within tertiary care settings, as opposed to patients under optometric review, is also useful for evaluating the generalizability of results to AMD populations.

This study aims to evaluate the predictive ability of RPE sinuosity for late AMD calculated from automatic, rather than manual, segmentations produced by proprietary software of a commercially available OCT device (Cirrus HD-OCT; Carl Zeiss Meditec, Dublin, CA, USA). It explores a metric that can be easily derived, is amenable to automation, and could potentially expand use of OCT-based biomarkers for clinical management, such as AMD prognostic models, as OCTs are widely utilized in clinical practice.20,21

sec.title: Study Population
sec.p: In this retrospective cohort study, consecutive participants attending three private ophthalmology clinics in metropolitan Sydney, New South Wales, Australia (Vision Eye Institute Drummoyne, Chatswood and Bondi Junction) between November 2, 2016 and December 10, 2023 (N = 9140) were reviewed until the target sample size for stable and progressor groups was reached. The stable group comprised of eyes with intermediate AMD (iAMD) without longitudinal progression to late AMD, whereas progressors converted to late AMD (geographic or neovascular AMD) within the study period. The inclusion criteria for both groups were age over 50 years, iAMD at the baseline visit, at least 2 visits during the study period, and availability of a macular cube OCT volume scan with B-scan quality score ≥6 at the first eligible visit. Patients with concomitant macular pathology or late AMD at baseline were excluded. The study eye was chosen randomly if both eyes met eligibility criteria.

A minimum sample size of 230 participants, with approximately one third converting to late AMD, was needed based on the numbers of stable and progressor eyes used in previous studies investigating OCT prognostic biomarkers in AMD.22,23 All participants consented to use of their de-identified data for research and the study was approved by the University of New South Wales Human Research Ethics Advisory Committee.

For all included participants, date of birth, sex, systemic disease status, axial length, visual acuity, the first consultation date with a diagnosis of iAMD, and most recent consultation with iAMD (non-progressors), or first visit where late AMD was diagnosed (progressors) during the study period, were extracted from clinical records and patient reports using practice management software, Bluechip (Health Communication Network Ltd, Sydney, Australia). Systemic conditions were defined as the presence or absence of cardiovascular disease or diabetes according to the International Classification of Diseases, 11th Revision,24 as there is some evidence systemic health can influence AMD pathogenesis.25–27

sec.title: Biomarker Identification
sec.p: In study eyes, outer retinal integrity was assessed quantitatively as RPE sinuosity and qualitatively as RPE and EZ continuity. EZ sinuosity was not assessed as there was no equivalent EZ band automatically segmented by the Cirrus HD-OCT software used in the ophthalmology clinics. The following AMD biomarkers currently used for prognosticating late disease were also assessed for comparative purposes: drusen volume (quantitatively assessed), pigmentary abnormalities, and SDD (qualitatively assessed). All OCT-based biomarkers were extracted from 512 × 128 macular volumes.

sec.title: Quantitative biomarkers
sec.p: Outer retinal band integrity was first quantified by calculating RPE sinuosity. The RPE line (segmentation of the actual RPE band) and RPE-fit line (estimation of the RPE band if it were free from any deformations) was automatically segmented by Cirrus software (Cirrus HD-OCT, Carl Zeiss Meditec, Dublin, CA, USA; Fig. 1).28 Participants with images showing deformed segmentations, processing errors, or significant artifacts were manually excluded (n = 2). The RPE-fit line was used as the reference in this study, as Bruch's membrane is not automatically segmented by proprietary software. The length of each line across the whole B-scan (6 mm) was extracted using custom MATLAB code, as per previous methodology18,19 and sinuosity was then calculated as a ratio of RPE:RPE-fit length. Smaller horizontal extents were not evaluated as this has not improved predictive performance previously.18

caption.p: Sinuosity calculated from the length of segmentation lines on foveal OCT B-scans for a stable (left column) and progressor (right column) iAMD eye. After extracting the raw scans (A, B, E, F), images were binarized to isolate the RPE (C, D, green) and RPE-fit line (G, H, purple).

sec.p: Foveal and aggregate RPE sinuosity was calculated from the foveal B-scan and averaging ratio values from the 5 central, horizontal B-scans, which were spaced 93.75 µm apart, including the maximum, median, and mean sinuosity values. The B-scan spacing was selected to ensure that large drusen would be captured (typically ≥145 µm29 in size with a mean diameter >300 µm on OCT),30 as well as other AMD lesions inducing outer retinal deformations, while minimizing image processing time. All sinuosity values were expressed as an absolute percentage from 1.0, that is, an RPE sinuosity of 0.5% represents an RPE:RPE line ratio of 1.005.

Drusen volume was extracted from the central 5 mm using advanced RPE analysis software (Cirrus HD-OCT; Carl Zeiss Meditec, Dublin, CA, USA). Drusen volume in the central 5 mm rather than 3 mm area was chosen to align more closely with the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) grid area used for Beckman AMD grading.31

sec.title: Qualitative biomarkers
sec.p: Outer retinal band integrity was also assessed qualitatively as EZ and RPE continuity by inspecting the central 1 mm of foveal B-scans and manually grading them as “disrupted” if the bands were discontinuous or absent and “intact” if the bands were continuous with homogeneous or heterogeneous reflectivity.2,4 All manual gradings were performed independently using baseline visit data by two experienced clinicians (authors A.D. and M.T.) with disagreements adjudicated by a senior retinal specialist (author S.C.).

Pigmentary abnormalities were manually graded as present or absent based on 200-degree ultrawide field imaging (Optomap, Daytona, Optos, UK), which shows excellent agreement with traditional 45 degrees color fundus photography.32 They were defined as hypo- or hyperpigmentation in eyes with drusen 63 µm or more in diameter without other associated retinal pathology.31 SDDs were manually graded from macular OCT scans (Cirrus HD-OCT; Carl Zeiss Meditec, Dublin, CA, USA) and considered present if ≥5 hyper-reflective mounds, or triangular lesions above the RPE, was observed in ≥1 B-scan within a macular OCT volume.33 All qualitative biomarkers were defined as present if graders were at least 90% certain that the biomarker was present, as per previously published methods.34

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: The primary outcomes of the study were the prognostic ability of RPE sinuosity, EZ, and RPE continuity, whereas the secondary outcomes were the odds of SDD, pigmentary abnormalities, and drusen volume for predicting late AMD. Independent and paired sample t-tests and Pearson's χ2 test were used to assess differences in continuous and categorical primary outcome measures, respectively. Differences in the RPE sinuosity, frequency of continuity measures, and other AMD biomarkers between stable and progressor groups were examined, with P values < 0.05 considered significant. To determine the independent predictive value of RPE sinuosity metrics, associations among demographic, ocular, and disease characteristics, including AMD biomarkers already used for prognostication, and RPE sinuosity were assessed using univariable and multivariable linear mixed-effects models. Then, the unadjusted and adjusted odds ratios (ORs), based on multiple regression results, of converting to late AMD within 2 and 5 years, and across the study period were calculated for sinuosity and continuity measures. The odds were only considered significant if the lower and upper confidence interval limits did not include 1, in line with currently accepted interpretations.35 RPE sinuosity was transformed into continuous and categorical variables to reflect current approaches for evaluating the prognostic ability of drusen volume in the literature, that is, odds of progression per 0.01 mm3 increase36,37 and if volume exceeds 0.03 mm3.38,39 The mean difference in sinuosity between groups was used to define the incremental units (i.e. odds per 0.5% ratio change) and threshold for categorization (odds if sinuosity was >100.5%) to determine the predictive ability of RPE sinuosity metrics.

All statistical analyses were performed using SPSS (version 25; IBM, Armonk, NY, USA). To assess the impact of missing axial length data in a high proportion of this retrospective cohort, Monte Carlo simulations were generated in MATLAB using epidemiological data derived from previous work on AMD populations identifying the minimum and maximum axial lengths of eyes with early to intermediate stage disease (21.7 to 27.5 mm).40 Intergrader agreement was also calculated for qualitatively assessed biomarkers using a 2-way mixed effects model of absolute agreement with good, moderate, and poor reliability defined as ≥0.75 to 0.9, 0.5 to 0.75, and <0.5.41

sec.title: Study Population
sec.p: A total of 267 participants were included in the study: 198 with stable intermediate AMD and 69 who developed late AMD. The mean follow-up time for all participants was 3.15 (1.93) years and ranged from 0.3 to 6.9 years, which was not statistically different between groups (P = 0.43). There was no significant difference in demographic variables between groups except for age, with progressors being slightly older at baseline (P = 0.03). All AMD biomarkers were present in a higher proportion of the progressor than stable eyes at baseline, including pigmentary abnormalities (χ2 = 0.001), SDD (χ2 > 0.0001), drusen volume (P = 0.01), and late AMD in the fellow eye (χ2 = 0.001; Table 1).

caption.p: Demographic, Ocular, and Disease Characteristics of Study Groups

fn.p: SD, standard deviation; AMD, age-related macular degeneration.
The P values (P) and χ2 < 0.05 were deemed significant and bolded.

sec.title: Whole Cohort
sec.p: Mean (SD) foveal sinuosity of all participants at baseline was 1.27% (1.24), whereas maximum, median, and mean foveal sinuosity across 5 central B-scans was 1.80% (1.50), 1.24% (1.20), and 1.26% (1.19), respectively. Outer retinal band integrity of the RPE and EZ at the fovea was discontinuous in 53.6% and 56.6% of participant eyes at baseline, respectively (Table 2).

caption.p: Comparison of Sinuosity and Continuity Between Groups (Absolute Sinuosity Values Expressed as Percentages)

fn.p: Sinuosity values expressed as a percentage difference from 100% as the theoretical value when there are no curvature anomalies induced by retinal pathology.

sec.title: Stable and Progressor Groups
sec.p: Differences bet-ween groups were assessed for those with 2 and 5 years of follow-up data and across the study period. No significant difference in baseline RPE sinuosity between groups followed for at least 2 or 5 years was found. Across the study period (up to 6.92 years), significant differences in all sinuosity measures were observed between groups (P = 0.02–0.04). The proportion of eyes with discontinuous RPE was not significantly different between stable versus the progressor participants followed for at least 2 years but was significant for 5-year follow-up and across the study period (P = 0.01). EZ continuity was not significantly different between groups for any time frame examined (see Table 2).

sec.title: Factors Impacting Sinuosity and Continuity Assessments
sec.p: Multivariable regression analysis was performed to identify factors influencing quantitative and qualitative outer retinal integrity measures. Foveal and aggregate RPE sinuosity (P < 0.0001 to P < 0.001) significantly decreased with age (P < 0.0001 to P < 0.001) and increased with higher drusen volume (P < 0.0001; Supplementary Tables S1, S2).

For RPE continuity, disruptions were inversely associated with visual acuity (P = 0.01), SDD presence and higher drusen volume (P < 0.001 and P = 0.03, respectively), whereas disrupted EZ was only associated with greater axial length (P = 0.01; see Supplementary Table S2). As 53.6% of the eyes were missing axial length values from incomplete retrospective records, 5 Monte Carlo simulations were generated using epidemiological data to verify the results.40 Repeating stepwise linear regression for each simulation found that the missing data had no significant effect on the associations between axial length and RPE sinuosity or EZ/RPE continuity (Supplementary Table S3), or classification rates for identifying participants who progressed within 2 years (Supplementary Table S4) and 5 years (Supplementary Table S5).

The intergrader reliability of qualitative biomarker assessments was also evaluated and found that gradings for EZ continuity were good intraclass correlation coefficient [ICC] = 0.751), moderate for SDD and pigment abnormalities (ICC = 0.610–0.721), and poor for RPE abnormalities (ICC = 0.481; Supplementary Table S7).

sec.title: Prognostic Ability of Outer Retinal Band and AMD Biomarkers
sec.p: Continuous and categorical measures of RPE sinuosity were defined by the difference in RPE sinuosity between stable and progressor eyes for foveal and aggregate measures, which was approximately 0.5% (see Table 2). Thus, the prognostic ability of RPE sinuosity (i.e. odds ratio of developing late AMD) was calculated for every 0.5% incremental increase in sinuosity above a reference value of 100% at baseline, as the continuous measure, and for baseline sinuosity greater than 100.5%, as the categorical measure.

The analysis showed that no outer integrity or AMD biomarkers could predict late AMD development within 2 years (Supplementary Table S6). In contrast, the odds of late AMD development within 5 years were significant per 0.5% increase in the foveal, median, and mean sinuosity (Fig. 2A, green data points with red asterisks), whereas maximum sinuosity, categorical measures of RPE sinuosity exceeding 0.5% (see Fig. 2A, dark blue data points), RPE, and EZ continuity (see Fig. 2A, purple data points) were insignificant predictors. Among current AMD biomarkers, only SDD demonstrated significant odds of progression (5.75 [1.12–32.61]; see Fig. 2A, pink data points).

caption.p: (A) The adjusted odds of progression to late AMD within 5 years for continuous (filled circles) and categorical (open circles) variables: outer retinal sinuosity (green and blue), continuity (purple), and AMD biomarkers (pink). Significant results denoted by red asterisks. (B) The adjusted odds of progression to late AMD within the study period for continuous (filled circles) and categorical (open circles) variables: outer retinal sinuosity (green = continuous and blue = categorical variables), continuity (purple), and AMD biomarkers (pink). Significant results denoted by red asterisks.

sec.p: Across the whole study period, the odds of progression to late disease were significant for all continuous (Fig. 2B, green data points) and categorical measures of RPE sinuosity (see Fig. 2B, dark blue data points with red asterisks), except maximum RPE sinuosity >0.5%. Neither RPE nor EZ continuity was successful and SDD was again the only other significant predictor (5.55 [1.88–16.39]).

The weak prognostic ability of drusen volume contrasts against previous work and thus a secondary analysis exploring performance when volume is extracted from the central 3 mm instead of 5 mm was conducted. This confirmed that drusen volume was not predictive of late AMD development within any time frame examined in this cohort, although there was a trend toward greater odds ratios for drusen volume >0.03 mm3. Larger increments of drusen volume increase, reflective of mean differences between stable and progressor groups, was also examined and similarly yielded insignificant results (see Supplementary Table S6).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study assessed the ability of a new, quantitative outer retinal integrity metric for predicting late AMD compared with current qualitative methods. We showed that higher RPE sinuosity of the foveal B-scan and aggregate median and mean sinuosity of B-scans from the central macular OCT volume significantly increased the risk of progression to late AMD, whereas qualitative continuity assessments were insignificant predictors. The findings support previous work showing that RPE sinuosity of the foveal B-scan is an independent, significant predictor of late AMD development, and improves its potential for clinical translation by reproducing these results from automatic segmentations of proprietary software. This may reduce variability in clinical assessments and enhance the accuracy of AMD prognosis in clinical settings.

sec.title: RPE Sinuosity Changes Are Less Pronounced Using Automated Versus Manual Segmentations
sec.p: Mean RPE sinuosity was significantly different between stable and progressor groups at baseline (approximately 0.5%) but less than previously reported values derived from manual segmentations (approximately 3%).19 This is likely due to the coarser fit of automatic versus manual RPE segmentations, which was verified by multiple graders in previous studies. Differences to previous studies may also result from using different ratio denominators, specifically automated RPE-fit line versus the manually segmented Bruch's membrane. The RPE-fit line was used as it was the closest approximation for Bruch's membrane, however, it is also an imperfect reference for sinuosity calculations as Cirrus HD-OCT software determines this on the assumption that there are no elevations when it can be distorted by outer retinal lesions, for example, drusen. Consequently, the RPE-fit line will be biased inward toward areas of RPE disturbance,42 resulting in RPE sinuosity underestimation. Indeed, the drusen volume of stable eyes was similar between this study and previous work investigating RPE sinuosity (0.03 mm3 and 0.02 mm3), yet foveal RPE sinuosity is much less at 1.16% vs. 2.8%. These findings suggest that RPE sinuosity should be calculated from segmentations of Bruch's membrane. Repeating the investigation using proprietary software (i.e. of a different OCT device) that provides such segmentations would be helpful for verifying the prognostic ability of RPE sinuosity.

sec.title: RPE Sinuosity Is Robust to Physiological Variations Affecting Other AMD Biomarkers
sec.p: RPE sinuosity was largely independent of demographic factors that affect other quantitative measurements used in AMD assessment, including drusen volume, RPE-BM complex thickness, and retinal thickness.43–45 Although robust to refraction, sex, and ethnicity, there was a significant, negative association between RPE sinuosity and age. Lack of adjustment for AMD lesions that cause RPE attenuation,46 namely incomplete outer retinal atrophy, could have contributed to this result. Identifying and adjusting for these lesions in the future, particularly in a tertiary care setting where high-risk patients are typically seen, would help confirm this hypothesis.

The EZ and RPE continuity were significantly affected by eye factors, axial length, and visual acuity, respectively. Longer axial length was associated with EZ disruption, likely due to the decreasing density of photoreceptors with increasing axial length,40,47 which may affect EZ visibility on OCT.48 Paradoxically, worse visual acuity was associated with continuous RPE. These results should be interpreted with caution given the suboptimal reliability of gradings, which may be impacted by variables affecting RPE band reflectivity, such as metabolic state49,50 and melanin deposition.51 Thus, RPE sinuosity appears more robust to physiological variation and subjective variability than other qualitative and quantitative AMD biomarkers examined in this study and is therefore worth exploring as an alternative outer retinal integrity metric.

sec.title: Associations to Drusen Volume Were Stronger for RPE Sinuosity Than Continuity Measures
sec.p: Only drusen volume significantly impacted RPE sinuosity, which increased with greater volume. The lack of association between RPE sinuosity to SDD and pigmentary abnormalities may be a side effect of the coarse (automatic) segmentations used to calculate sinuosity, which could have attenuated associations to more subtle biomarkers. Even with manual segmentations, associations of SDD and pigmentary abnormalities to RPE sinuosity were not consistent.18,19 These results indicate that the prognostic ability of RPE sinuosity could be further enhanced by including other key AMD biomarkers in prediction models if they can be measured reliably.

RPE continuity was significantly associated with drusen volume and SDD. However, an inverse effect was observed, that is, SDD presence and higher drusen volume were associated with continuous RPE. Using only one B-scan to assess continuity could explain these results, as SDD is more prevalent away from the foveal region toward the parafovea52,53 and, similarly, drusen affecting non-foveal B-scans could be missed. EZ continuity was not significantly associated with any AMD lesion, which could be due to the relatively distal location of the EZ band from the RPE and, consequently, lower induction of sinuosity changes from drusen and other AMD lesions which primarily impact the RPE. These results shed doubt on the usefulness of continuity measures for AMD prognosis, particularly in resource-constrained settings where multiple and/or highly skilled raters may be unavailable to verify gradings.

sec.title: RPE Sinuosity Is Superior to Outer Retinal Continuity for Late AMD Prediction
sec.p: Within a 5-year time frame, continuous RPE sinuosity metrics were significant predictors of late AMD. Specifically, every 0.5% increase in foveal, median, and mean RPE sinuosity was associated with a 130%, 152%, and 129% increase in risk of progression to late AMD, respectively. Within the study period, both continuous and categorical measures of RPE sinuosity (exceeding 100.5%) were significant predictors, associated with 89% to 135% increased odds of conversion. In contrast, neither RPE nor EZ continuity could predict progression to late AMD within any time frame examined. This was surprising as both are considered strong prognostic biomarkers in the literature.1 Coarser gradings for pigmentary abnormalities, drusen volume >0.03 mm3, and categorical sinuosity metrics could reduce the sensitivity of these biomarkers for predicting progression versus quantitative metrics, despite significant differences between groups (see Tables 1, 2). Relying on only one B-scan and variability in subjective grading may have also contributed to the underwhelming prognostic performance. Varying the assessment zone beyond the central 1 mm is a possibility, however, this will likely dilute the sensitivity of the metric for AMD outcomes as AMD lesions, such as drusen and geographic atrophy,54,55 are typically concentrated in the central 1 mm area.

The prognostic value of drusen volume was also examined for comparative purposes, as it is a commonly used quantitative metric for AMD prognosis. Neither continuous (per 0.01 mm3 increase) nor categorical drusen volume (≥0.03 mm3) variables calculated were significant predictors of late AMD within any time frame, which contrasts against previous reports.1,19 Again, coarser segmentation of RPE and Bruch's membrane using proprietary algorithms could be a contributing factor. Indeed, drusen volume calculated from automatic versus manually adjusted segmentations is lower and also for Cirrus versus Spectralis OCT devices.56 The possibility that the extraction diameter of the drusen volume impacted the prognostic ability was examined by conducting a secondary analysis of ORs for drusen volume within the central 3 mm. Although there was a trend toward higher ORs for volumes >0.03 mm3, this did not reach significance. Similarly, using increments of 0.02 mm3 to define continuous drusen volume did not improve prognostic ability. It may be worth exploring drusen volume metrics derived from the central 500 µm to better match the vertical extent of B-scan extraction used to calculate sinuosity metrics in future work.

Out of the qualitative biomarkers currently used for late AMD risk assessment, only SDD could predict progression, which was associated with a 475% and 455% increase in odds of developing late disease within 5 years and across the study period. It was also unable to predict short-term conversion to late disease within 2 years. SDD is already recognized as a strong prognostic biomarker and these results reinforce the importance of its inclusion in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study (AREDS) simplified severity scale.57 The results also suggest that quantitative OCT metrics of outer retinal integrity could complement existing prognostic biomarkers used in late AMD risk models.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: Due to the retrospective nature of the study, some demographic and eye variables in the data set were not complete. Namely, axial length data were missing in approximately half the participants. However, mean axial length in our study was similar to values reported for non-exudative AMD populations in the literature.58 We also used Monte Carlo simulations to further test associations between outer retinal integrity measures and axial length, which showed similar findings to the original analysis (see Supplementary Table S3) and suggests that the missing data did not significantly bias our findings. This could be further confirmed by performing the investigation on a prospective cohort to minimize missing data. The results presented also do not assess outer retinal sinuosity as a classifier for stable versus future progressors, which is typically evaluated using area under the curve sensitivity or specificity.59 Rather, they highlight its potential for improving existing models currently used for predicting late AMD risk as a quantitative, easily calculated metric that requires minimal data processing. A subanalysis of outer retinal sinuosity in converters with geographic atrophy versus neovascular late AMD should also be considered in future work investigating cohorts with adequate sample sizes.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This study found that RPE sinuosity metrics outperformed qualitative assessments of outer retinal integrity for predicting late AMD. Moreover, they were derived from automatic segmentations which are readily produced from proprietary OCT software. Thus, RPE sinuosity represents an accessible, quantitative prognostic biomarker of late AMD, which is robust to inter- and intra-observer variability and could improve disease monitoring and management in clinical practice. Verification of these findings using other OCT devices in a prospective cohort is needed to determine whether RPE sinuosity can enhance late AMD risk models and its potential for improving clinical management.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: Supported by the Future Vision Foundation, the Australian Government Research Training Program and, in part, by funding from the National Health and Medical Research Council (NHMRC #1174385 and #1186915). The authors would like to acknowledge Associate Professor Sieu Khuu and Dr David Chan (UNSW Stats Central service) for providing statistical analysis assistance.

Disclosure: R. Cheung, None; M. Trinh, None; A. Duong, None; S. Chen, None; D. Ng, None; G. Schiller, None; C. Hodge, Future Vision Foundation (S), which provided funding for this project. The funding body had no influence on any aspect of the study design or interpretation. reporting; L. Nivison-Smith, None
